stories.headline: Iran rejects US ceasefire plan and submits five-point counter-proposal as Israel strikes Isfahan
stories.body: Iran formally rejected the 15-point US ceasefire plan delivered via Pakistan, with an anonymous official quoted on state TV saying Tehran would end the war when it decides to do so and on its own conditions. Foreign minister Abbas Araghchi said the proposals had been passed to senior authorities but Iran had no intention of negotiating for now. Tehran submitted its own five-point counter-proposal demanding an end to fighting and assassinations of its officials, guarantees against future aggression, reparations, and Iranian control over the Strait of Hormuz. Trump insisted at a Washington fundraiser that Iran still wanted a deal but was afraid to say so publicly. China's foreign minister urged dialogue, telling Egypt a glimmer of hope for peace had emerged. Meanwhile, Israel's military said it had completed a wide-scale wave of strikes targeting infrastructure in several areas across Iran, including Isfahan. The US military said its forces had now hit more than 10,000 targets and destroyed 92 percent of the Iranian navy's largest vessels.

stories.headline: UN votes to recognise enslavement of Africans as 'gravest crime against humanity'
stories.body: The United Nations General Assembly voted 123 to 3 to describe the transatlantic chattel slave trade as the gravest crime against humanity and called for reparations as a concrete step towards remedying historical wrongs. The resolution, proposed by Ghana and backed by the African Union and the Caribbean Community, was opposed only by the United States, Israel, and Argentina, while 52 countries including the UK and EU member states abstained. Ghana's president John Mahama told the assembly that when history beckoned, they did what was right for the memory of millions who suffered the indignity of slavery. The UK said it disagreed with fundamental propositions of the text and was firmly of the view that no single set of atrocities should be regarded as more or less significant than another. The resolution, while not legally binding, carries the weight of global opinion and calls on member states to consider apologising and contributing to a reparations fund.

stories.headline: Zelenskyy says US has linked security guarantees to ceding all of Donbas as Ukraine strikes Russian Baltic ports
stories.body: Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy told Reuters the United States is making its offer of security guarantees conditional on Kyiv ceding all of eastern Donbas to Russia, saying Trump was applying pressure to end the four-year war while focused on Iran. Zelenskyy also accused Russia of trying to blackmail Washington by offering to stop sharing military intelligence with Iran in exchange for the US cutting off intelligence to Ukraine. Separately, Ukraine launched its largest aerial attack of the year, striking the Baltic port of Ust-Luga in the Gulf of Finland, one of Russia's main oil export terminals, setting the facility ablaze. It was the second Russian Baltic port hit in three days after strikes on Primorsk on Monday. Russia said it intercepted 389 Ukrainian drones across several regions, while nearly half a million people lost electricity in Belgorod region after Ukrainian strikes damaged energy infrastructure.

stories.headline: Sarah Mullally enthroned as first female Archbishop of Canterbury
stories.body: Sarah Mullally was formally installed as archbishop of Canterbury on Wednesday, becoming the first woman to lead the Church of England in its nearly 500-year history. The former nurse took her seat on the 13th-century Chair of St Augustine at Canterbury Cathedral before 2,000 guests including Prince William, his wife Catherine, and Prime Minister Keir Starmer. In her inaugural sermon, Mullally prayed for peace in the Middle East, Ukraine, Sudan, and Myanmar, and acknowledged the suffering caused by the church's past safeguarding failures that led to her predecessor Justin Welby's resignation in 2024. She wore a cope fastened with a clasp modelled on the belt from her nursing days and a ring given to a predecessor by Pope Paul VI in 1966. The Church of England ordained its first female priests in 1994 and its first female bishop in 2015.

stories.headline: Italy's tourism minister resigns as Meloni's government shaken by referendum defeat
stories.body: Italy's tourism minister Daniela Santanche resigned on Wednesday after Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni took the unprecedented step of publicly calling for her to go, as the government reels from a bruising defeat in a constitutional referendum on judicial reform. Santanche, who faces legal proceedings over allegations of fraud and false accounting, initially resisted for 24 hours before conceding in a letter laced with bitterness, saying she had not wanted to be made a scapegoat for the referendum loss. Two other figures also fell: Andrea Delmastro, the justice ministry's number two, resigned after revelations he had held restaurant shares with links to the Camorra, and the justice minister's chief of staff Giusi Bartolozzi stepped down amid her own legal troubles. The cascade of resignations marked the most turbulent period for Meloni since she took office in 2022, with opposition parties calling it an evident political crisis.

stories.headline: Meta and YouTube found liable in landmark Los Angeles social media addiction trial
stories.body: A Los Angeles jury found Meta and YouTube intentionally built addictive platforms that harmed a young woman's mental health, awarding her six million dollars in damages in a verdict likely to have implications for hundreds of similar cases. Jurors determined the companies acted with malice, oppression, or fraud, assigning Meta 70 percent of the liability and Google 30 percent. The verdict came one day after a separate New Mexico jury ordered Meta to pay 375 million dollars for endangering children, marking the first time juries in two US states have sided against social media companies over child safety in a single week. Tech watchdog groups declared the era of Big Tech invincibility over. Meta and Google both said they disagreed with the verdict and would appeal. During his testimony in February, Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg had relied on the company's policy of not allowing users under 13, despite internal research showing young children were using its platforms.

stories.headline: ICE agents begin checking IDs at airport security lines as shutdown-related delays worsen
stories.body: Federal immigration agents have begun checking identification documents at TSA security checkpoints in several major US airports including JFK, Atlanta's Hartsfield-Jackson, and Houston's Bush Intercontinental, acting on Trump's directive. The deployment comes as thousands of TSA agents have been working without pay since Congress failed to renew Department of Homeland Security funding last month, contributing to mounting delays. Houston's Bush Airport has become a symbol of the shutdown's impact on air travel, with wait times far exceeding those at other airports. A video from San Francisco Airport showed federal officers arresting a crying woman, while a US appeals court separately sided with the Trump administration on detaining immigrants without bond hearings. In New Jersey, the governor signed a law limiting face coverings for law enforcement including ICE agents during operations.

stories.headline: SpaceX reportedly preparing to file for stock market listing in what could be history's largest IPO
stories.body: Shares of space companies soared on Wednesday after The Information reported that Elon Musk's SpaceX could file within days to list shares on the stock market, with a valuation of around 1.75 trillion dollars. The potential initial public offering could raise more than 75 billion dollars, making it the largest stock market debut in history. Rocket Lab and Firefly Aerospace shares rose more than 10 percent, while satellite maker Sidus Space gained nearly 19 percent and Intuitive Machines jumped almost 15 percent. SpaceX has become an aerospace powerhouse securing billions in US government contracts. An IPO could make Musk, whose current fortune exceeds 820 billion dollars according to Forbes, the world's first trillionaire.

stories.headline: Sanders and Ocasio-Cortez push for moratorium on AI data centres until safeguards in place
stories.body: Senator Bernie Sanders and Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez introduced legislation to pause the construction of new AI data centres until national safeguards are established to protect workers, civil liberties, and the environment. Sanders said lawmakers were way behind in their understanding of AI and that a handful of billionaire Big Tech oligarchs should not be allowed to make decisions reshaping the economy and democracy. At least 36 data centres were blocked or delayed across the US between May 2024 and June 2025, disrupting 162 billion dollars in investment, with opposition cutting across partisan lines. A recent NBC poll found 57 percent of voters believed AI risks outweigh benefits. The bill faces an uphill battle in the Republican-controlled Congress, with even some Democrats dismissing it, including Senator John Fetterman who called it a China First approach.

stories.headline: Philippines transport workers strike as Iran war fuel crisis chokes Asia's economies
stories.body: Hundreds of transport workers in Manila went on strike over fuel prices that have more than doubled since the Iran war began on February 28, as the Philippines remains under a national energy emergency declared last week. Jeepney drivers, motorcycle riders, and car ride-hailing workers gathered across the capital holding signs demanding the government scrap fuel taxes and introduce state price controls. One 62-year-old driver told the BBC he had no food for his five children and had received no government aid despite queuing for more than five hours. President Ferdinand Marcos said the country was working to secure new sources of oil. The strikes reflect a broader pattern across Asia, where the Iran war's impact on fuel supplies is upending everyday life and prompting governments to consider emergency measures.

stories.headline: Sanchez invokes Iraq war in heated congressional clash with Feijoo over Iran conflict
stories.body: Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez launched a fierce attack on the PP and Vox in Congress on Wednesday, accusing them of cowardice and complicity for their silence on the Iran war and drawing pointed parallels with Jose Maria Aznar's decision to join the Iraq war in 2003. Sanchez recalled marching against that war and said Spain would not be an accomplice this time. Feijoo responded without mentioning Trump by name, telling Sanchez he was a sham pacifist who had tripled arms purchases from the United States, and brandishing the image of an Iranian propaganda missile bearing Sanchez's face. Vox leader Santiago Abascal accused Sanchez of exploiting wars and crises for political gain. The debate took place as the government secured the votes to pass its emergency economic decree on Thursday, with Junts confirming its support for the package of 80 measures to cushion the war's economic impact.

stories.headline: Storm Therese batters Canary Islands with historic flooding, forcing evacuations and UME deployment
stories.body: The Canary Islands are enduring their worst storm in recent memory as Storm Therese enters its eighth day, dumping over 700 litres per square metre in parts of Gran Canaria and triggering evacuations across Tenerife and Gran Canaria. The government deployed the Military Emergency Unit to both islands after torrential rains overwhelmed emergency services, generating 1,783 incidents. Tenerife was elevated to red alert for extraordinary danger after 60 millimetres fell in a single hour near Tacoronte, while dozens of dams in Gran Canaria reached capacity and had to release water. Schools were suspended and the ES-Alert mobile warning system was activated. Emergency officials said no one anywhere on the planet could have predicted convective cells of such virulence. No deaths have been reported despite the scale of the flooding.

stories.headline: Spain's foreign minister visits Algeria to rebuild ties and secure gas supplies amid Iran war
stories.body: Foreign minister Jose Manuel Albares travelled to Algiers on Thursday for the first high-level visit since Spain's 2022 diplomatic rupture with Algeria over its support for Morocco's position on Western Sahara. The visit is driven by energy security: Algeria was Spain's largest gas supplier in 2025 at roughly 35 percent of imports, and the government and Naturgy are considering increasing volumes through the Medgaz pipeline by more than 12 percent after gas prices surged 60 percent since the Iran war began. Albares will also meet the minister of hydrocarbons and mines and Spanish companies operating in Algeria. Trade relations have recovered dramatically, with Spanish exports to Algeria reaching 2.13 billion euros in 2025, a 270 percent increase from 2024, after Algiers lifted the commercial restrictions it had imposed in retaliation for Spain's Sahara policy shift.

stories.headline: Jihadist cell leader planning lone-wolf attack arrested in Palma de Mallorca
stories.body: Spanish National Police arrested the leader of a jihadist cell in Palma de Mallorca who was planning a large-scale lone-wolf terrorist attack on Spanish soil. The suspect had been sharing Daesh propaganda on social media and had expressed his desire to travel to conflict zones for jihad. Two other members of the network were arrested simultaneously in Tangier, Morocco, in a joint operation between Spain's intelligence services and Morocco's security directorate. The Tangier suspects were financing and providing logistical support to Daesh fighters in the Sahel and Somalia. The arrested man had been living for two months in a shared flat on Carrer Gabriel Maura, where his four flatmates reportedly had no suspicion of his activities. He was transferred by helicopter to Madrid to appear before the Audiencia Nacional. The operation is the latest in a strategic partnership between Spain and Morocco that has dismantled more than 30 cells since 2014.

stories.headline: Court freezes accounts of consultancy that paid Zapatero in Plus Ultra corruption case
stories.body: An investigating judge at Spain's Audiencia Nacional has ordered the freezing of bank accounts belonging to Analisis Relevante, the consultancy at the centre of the Plus Ultra airline corruption case that paid former prime minister Jose Luis Rodriguez Zapatero roughly 460,000 euros over five years for advisory work. The consultancy's sole administrator, Julio Martinez Martinez, was arrested late last year along with Plus Ultra executives. A second company linked to Martinez, Agropecuaria Lucena, has also had its accounts frozen, along with a property in Almeria connected to Venezuelan businessmen. The case centres on the government's controversial 53-million-euro rescue of the airline. Zapatero said on Monday that all his work was legal and that he did not know the details of the relationship between the consultancy and Plus Ultra. The judge has asked for details of a related investigation that was archived in 2023.

stories.headline: Police evict 126 people from Sagrera shantytown as social services struggle to offer alternatives
stories.body: Barcelona's Guardia Urbana and the Mossos d'Esquadra carried out a joint operation on Wednesday morning to clear a sprawling shantytown of 126 people living in makeshift shacks beside the Pont del Treball Digne in the Sagrera neighbourhood, on land owned by the rail infrastructure company Adif. The settlement had existed for about two years, housing mostly men who collect scrap metal, along with some families but no minors. The city said the eviction was prompted by a fire brigade report warning of grave and imminent risk after a small fire the previous Saturday. Of the 126 people displaced, only 58 agreed to speak with social services. Advocacy groups denounced the operation for being carried out without prior notice and without housing alternatives. One young man told TV3 he did not know where he would sleep and planned to find a tent to avoid sleeping in the open.

stories.headline: Sant Agusti eviction suspended after overnight neighbourhood resistance; Govern threatens millionaire fine
stories.body: The first attempted eviction at the contested Sant Agusti building in Gracia was suspended on Wednesday after hundreds of neighbours held an overnight resistance vigil, and the Catalan government announced it would open a sanctions file against the Dutch owner, New Amsterdam Developers, potentially resulting in a millionaire fine. The company had purchased the building in 2023, declined to renew existing leases, and illegally converted flats into coliving rooms at nearly 1,000 euros per room, roughly triple previous rents. Barcelona's housing commissioner called the eviction inadmissible and the company's practices speculative. The city had already fined the firm 20,000 euros for exceeding its renovation permit. Organisers hope to replicate the success of the Casa Orsola campaign, which forced the conversion of a speculative building to affordable housing.

stories.headline: Barcelona scrambles to redistribute interurban buses as fuel prices and Rodalies crisis overwhelm metro stations
stories.body: Barcelona is studying how to reorganize interurban bus routes entering the city along the Meridiana avenue as soaring fuel prices and the ongoing Rodalies commuter rail crisis push record numbers of passengers onto public transport, overwhelming metro stations at Fabra i Puig and La Sagrera. Loudspeaker warnings now ask passengers to spread out along platforms as crowds pour in from buses. The city is considering extending some bus routes further south to the Clot metro station to relieve pressure, though ongoing roadworks on the Meridiana complicate the plan. The neighbourhood federation FAVB said it was essential to respond now while people were switching from cars, so they do not return to driving. TMB will add an extra train to the L5 metro line after Easter and plans to reinforce the L1 as soon as possible.

stories.headline: 200-metre Civil War air raid shelter discovered during street works in Sants-Montjuic
stories.body: Mossos d'Esquadra and Barcelona's archaeology service accessed a Civil War air raid shelter roughly 200 metres long beneath Carrer del 26 de Gener de 1641 in the Sants-Montjuic district, after construction workers carrying out street renovations uncovered multiple access points since early March. Initial inspection suggests the shelter was built in 1938 and saw prolonged use, with latrines connected to running water, remnants of electrical installations, intact water jugs, and small alcoves carved into the walls that may have served as cupboards. The lead archaeologist described it as a fairly large shelter built with care, though one section excavated directly into clay was in poor condition with structural collapse risks that prevented further exploration. Technicians will now determine whether two additional access points belong to the same shelter or separate structures.

stories.headline: Barcelona, Tarragona, and Granollers launch 100-day countdown to Tour de France Grand Depart
stories.body: Barcelona, Tarragona, and Granollers will hold simultaneous celebrations on Thursday to mark 100 days until the Tour de France begins in Catalunya on July 4, the first time the race has started in Spain. The Passeig Lluis Companys in Barcelona will host a party featuring the activation of a countdown clock and a concert by Doctor Prats performing the official Grand Depart anthem. Tarragona will stage street circus performances with bicycles as the centrepiece, while Granollers will unveil a giant yellow maillot. The Torre Glories observation deck in Barcelona has already been illuminated in the race's signature yellow. The three Catalan cities will host the opening three stages before the Tour heads into France.
